## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*List any six categories from the OWASP Mobile Top 10 and give a one-line mitigation for each.*

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

| # | OWASP Category | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | M1 – Improper Credential Usage | Store credentials in **Android Keystore / iOS Keychain**; never hard-code. |
| 2 | M3 – Insecure Authentication | Use **OAuth 2.0 with PKCE**; enforce server-side session validation. |
| 3 | M5 – Insecure Communication | Force **TLS 1.2+** + **Certificate Pinning**; disable cleartext. |
| 4 | M7 – Insufficient Binary Protections | Apply **R8/ProGuard** obfuscation and **SafetyNet** attestation. |
| 5 | M9 – Insecure Data Storage | Use **EncryptedSharedPreferences** or **SQLCipher** (AES-256). |
| 6 | M10 – Insufficient Cryptography | Use **AES-GCM** for symmetric, **RSA-2048+** for asymmetric, `SecureRandom` for nonces. |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the CIA Triad in the context of mobile app security with a one-line example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Confidentiality (C):** Ensuring only the legitimate user can read their private chat messages — *e.g., encrypted with AES-256 in SQLCipher.*
- **Integrity (I):** Ensuring the app binary or API response is not modified in transit — *e.g., TLS 1.3 + HMAC-SHA256 payload signing.*
- **Availability (A):** Ensuring the app and backend remain usable — *e.g., rate-limiting + WAF + cloud autoscaling.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> Two fully independent alternative questions are provided. KTU examiners always offer an internal choice within a module.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **OWASP Mobile Top 10** in detail. For any **five** categories, describe the threat and its specific mitigation strategy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **Defense-in-Depth** architecture for securing a mobile application. List the layers and state which OWASP category each layer primarily addresses. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

The **OWASP Mobile Top 10 (2023/2024)** is the industry-standard list of the most critical mobile application security risks:

1. **M1 – Improper Credential Usage:** Hard-coding API keys, storing passwords in `SharedPreferences`, or transmitting credentials over plain HTTP. *Mitigation:* Use the **OS Keystore/Keychain**, enforce short-lived tokens, and never embed secrets in source code.

2. **M3 – Insecure Authentication / Authorization:** Performing auth checks only on the client side, missing session expiry, or allowing direct object references. *Mitigation:* Use **OAuth 2.0 with PKCE**, server-side session validation, and rotate JWTs every **≤ 900 seconds**.

3. **M5 – Insecure Communication:** Apps using HTTP, ignoring certificate errors, or using self-signed certs. *Mitigation:* Enforce **TLS 1.2+**, enable **Certificate Pinning** via `OkHttp CertificatePinner`, set `usesCleartextTraffic="false"`.

4. **M7 – Insufficient Binary Protections:** Apps easily reverse-engineered with APKTool, Frida, or Jadx. *Mitigation:* Apply **R8/ProGuard** obfuscation, **SafetyNet Attestation**, anti-debugging checks, and use **APK Signature Scheme v3**.

5. **M9 – Insecure Data Storage:** Plaintext SQLite databases, world-readable files, or unencrypted cache. *Mitigation:* Use **SQLCipher (AES-256)** for databases and **EncryptedSharedPreferences** for small key-value pairs.

6. **M10 – Insufficient Cryptography:** Using deprecated algorithms (DES, MD5, SHA-1) or `java.util.Random` for IV/nonce generation. *Mitigation:* Use **AES-GCM-256** for symmetric encryption, **RSA-2048+** for asymmetric, **SHA-256** for hashing, and **`java.security.SecureRandom`** for randomness.

*(Any 5 of the above with explanation – 5 × 1.4 = 7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** is a layered security strategy where multiple independent controls protect the asset, so a single breach does not compromise the system.

| Layer | Control | Primary OWASP Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 – Network** | TLS 1.3 + Certificate Pinning | M5 |
| **L2 – Device Integrity** | Root/Jailbreak detection, SafetyNet | M7, M8 |
| **L3 – App Binary** | R8 obfuscation, anti-debug, code signing | M7 |
| **L4 – Data at Rest** | EncryptedSharedPreferences, SQLCipher | M9 |
| **L5 – Data in Transit / IPC** | Encrypted Intents, secure ContentProviders | M5, M4 |
| **L6 – Authentication** | OAuth 2.0 + PKCE + biometric | M1, M3 |
| **L7 – Business Logic** | Server-side validation, rate limiting, audit logs | M3, M4, M6 |

*(Diagram: same as §4.3 in this notes file – 3 marks for diagram, 4 marks for layer explanation and OWASP mapping.)*

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 3]**

**(a)** What is **STRIDE** threat modeling? Apply STRIDE to identify threats in a **mobile banking app** scenario and map each threat to its OWASP Mobile Top 10 category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the following secure-storage mechanisms with examples:
  (i) Android **EncryptedSharedPreferences**
  (ii) iOS **Keychain**
  (iii) **SQLCipher** for relational data. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**STRIDE** is a Microsoft-developed threat-classification mnemonic with six categories (derived from §3.1):

| STRIDE | Applied to Mobile Banking App | OWASP Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **S – Spoofing** | Attacker impersonates the user's login on another device using stolen credentials | M1 (Credentials), M3 (Auth) |
| **T – Tampering** | Modifying APK binary, injecting malicious code, or altering transaction amount in transit | M7 (Binary), M4 (Input Validation) |
| **R – Repudiation** | User denies initiating a fund transfer; no server-side audit log exists | M8 (Misconfiguration), M3 |
| **I – Information Disclosure** | Credit card details or OTP read from insecure SharedPreferences | M9 (Storage), M5 (Communication) |
| **D – Denial of Service** | Flooding the banking API with millions of requests to block legitimate users | M3 (Auth), M8 |
| **E – Elevation of Privilege** | Rooted device bypasses root-detection, granting attacker full app control | M7 (Binary), M8 |

*(6 categories × 1 mark each ≈ 6 marks, plus 1 mark for the introductory STRIDE definition.)*

---

##### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**(i) Android EncryptedSharedPreferences** *(2 marks)*
- Provided by **AndroidX Security** (`androidx.security:security-crypto`).
- Wraps `SharedPreferences` with **AES-256-GCM** for values and **AES-256-SIV** for keys.
- Master key is stored in the **Android Keystore** (hardware-backed on devices with TEE/StrongBox).
- Example use: storing OAuth refresh tokens or biometric session keys.

**(ii) iOS Keychain** *(2.5 marks)*
- Apple's encrypted key-value store, protected by the device's Secure Enclave.
- Items tagged with `kSecClass`, `kSecAttrService`, `kSecAttrAccessible` attributes.
- Recommended accessibility flag: **`kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly`** (no iCloud sync, no backup).
- Used for passwords, certificates, and cryptographic keys.

**(iii) SQLCipher** *(2.5 marks)*
- Open-source extension to SQLite providing **AES-256 full-database encryption**.
- Transparent to the application — drop-in replacement for `SQLiteOpenHelper`.
- Encryption key derived via **PBKDF2** with high iteration count ($\geq 100{,}000$).
- Used for storing chat histories, transaction logs, or offline user data.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (verified from past KTU valuation scripts):**
>
> 1. **Confusing OWASP Web Top 10 with Mobile Top 10** — listing *XSS* or *CSRF* as primary mobile threats costs you full marks. Mobile-specific risks include **M1 (credentials)**, **M7 (binary)**, and **M9 (storage)**.
> 2. **Citing MD5 / DES / SHA-1 as "secure"** — examiners immediately deduct 1–2 marks; always state **AES-256, SHA-256, RSA-2048**.
> 3. **Recommending `java.util.Random`** — must be `java.security.SecureRandom`.
> 4. **Saying "use HTTPS" without specifying version** — always say **TLS 1.2+** (preferred **TLS 1.3**) and mention **Certificate Pinning**.
> 5. **Forgetting the diagram in DiD questions** — without a labelled diagram, expect a **2–3 mark penalty** even if textual explanation is perfect.
> 6. **No mention of OWASP in any answer** — examiners expect at least one reference; absence is treated as incomplete coverage.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE**

- ✅ **OWASP Mobile Top 10 (2023/2024)** — Know all 10 IDs by heart; can list M1 → M10 with mitigations.
- ✅ **CIA Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.
- ✅ **STRIDE** — Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info Disclosure, DoS, Elevation.
- ✅ **Cryptography Constants** — **AES-256**, **RSA-2048**, **SHA-256**, **TLS 1.2/1.3**, **PBKDF2 ≥ 100k**.
- ✅ **Secure Storage Tools** — **EncryptedSharedPreferences**, **Keychain**, **SQLCipher**, **Android Keystore**.
- ✅ **Binary Protection Tools** — **R8/ProGuard**, **SafetyNet Attestation**, **APK Signature Scheme v3**.
- ✅ **Authentication Standard** — **OAuth 2.0 with PKCE**, short-lived **JWT (≤ 900s)**.
- ✅ **Risk Formula** — $R = L \times I$, where $L, I \in [1, 5]$, so $R \in [1, 25]$.
- ✅ **CVSS Range** — $0.0$ (none) → $10.0$ (critical).
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth Layers** — L1 Network → L7 Business Logic (draw the diagram if asked).
- ✅ **Never use:** `java.util.Random`, MD5, SHA-1, DES, 3DES, ECB mode, cleartext HTTP, plaintext SharedPreferences.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing Secure Authentication and Data Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT753 - MOBILE APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Implementing Secure Authentication and Data Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Secure Authentication — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Secure Authentication** is the process of rigorously verifying the identity of a user, device, or system entity in a mobile application using cryptographically hardened mechanisms that resist impersonation, replay, man-in-the-middle (MITM), and credential-stuffing attacks, while ensuring confidentiality, integrity, and non-repudiation of the claimed identity.

In the context of Android (KTU 2024 Scheme - PECDT753), Secure Authentication encompasses:
- **Local Authentication** — BiometricPrompt, PIN/Pattern, Device Credential
- **Token-Based Authentication** — JWT (JSON Web Tokens), OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect
- **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** — Combining 2 or more of *Knowledge*, *Possession*, and *Inherence* factors
- **Certificate-Based Authentication** — Mutual TLS (mTLS), X.509 client certificates

## 1.2 Secure Data Storage — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Secure Data Storage** refers to the systematic application of cryptographic primitives (encryption, hashing, salting) combined with platform-level sandboxing mechanisms to protect sensitive user and application data from unauthorized access, both **at rest** (on device) and **in transit** (over the network).

For mobile platforms, secure storage layers include:
- **Android Keystore System** — Hardware-backed cryptographic key storage
- **EncryptedSharedPreferences** — AES-256 encrypted key-value storage (Jetpack Security)
- **EncryptedFile** — Stream-based encrypted file I/O
- **SQLCipher** — AES-256 encrypted SQLite database
- **Room DB with Tink encryption** — Database column-level encryption

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault" Model

Imagine your mobile app is a **high-security bank vault** (Secure Data Storage), and the entrance has a **multi-stage identity checkpoint** (Secure Authentication).

| Real-World Analogy | Mobile Security Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Bank Vault Door | Android Keystore (hardware-isolated key storage) |
| Photo ID Check | Username/Password Authentication |
| Fingerprint Scanner | BiometricPrompt (Biometric factor) |
| OTP sent to phone | TOTP / SMS-based 2FA (Possession factor) |
| Locked safety deposit box | EncryptedSharedPreferences / EncryptedFile |
| CCTV recording everything | Audit Logs & Certificate Pinning |
| Security guard checking signatures | JWT Signature Verification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Authentication answers *"Who are you?"* while Data Storage answers *"How do we protect what you store?"* — both are inseparable pillars of mobile security architecture.

## 1.4 Standard Security Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **AES-256** — Block size: **128 bits**, Key size: **256 bits** (industry standard for symmetric encryption)
- **RSA-2048** — Asymmetric key length: **2048 bits** (minimum secure)
- **SHA-256** — Hash output: **256 bits** (collision-resistant)
- **BCrypt Cost Factor** — Recommended: **12** (≈ 250 ms per hash on modern hardware)
- **JWT Lifetime** — Access token: **15 minutes**, Refresh token: **7–30 days**
- **TLS 1.3** — Recommended transport security protocol (deprecates TLS 1.0/1.1)
- **Android Target SDK 34** — Mandates use of crypto-safe APIs
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Authentication

### Pillar 1 — Authentication Factors
A robust system relies on a combination of the following:

- **Factor 1: Knowledge (Something you know)** — Password, PIN, security question
- **Factor 2: Possession (Something you have)** — OTP via SMS, hardware token, smartphone
- **Factor 3: Inherence (Something you are)** — Fingerprint, Face ID, Iris, Voice
- **Factor 4: Location (Somewhere you are)** — GPS-based, IP geolocation
- **Factor 5: Behavior (Something you do)** — Typing pattern, gait analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **2FA (Two-Factor Authentication)** combines any **two** distinct factors. **MFA** combines **three or more**. KTU examiners often test the *definition* of 2FA vs MFA — do not confuse them.

### Pillar 2 — Hashing vs Encryption

| Property | Hashing | Encryption |
|---|---|---|
| Reversible? | **No** (one-way) | **Yes** (with key) |
| Output size | Fixed (e.g., 256 bits for SHA-256) | Variable (ciphertext size) |
| Key required? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Use case | Password storage, integrity check | Data confidentiality |
| Example algorithms | SHA-256, BCrypt, Argon2 | AES-256, RSA-2048, ChaCha20 |

### Pillar 3 — Symmetric vs Asymmetric Cryptography

- **Symmetric Encryption** uses the **same key** for encryption and decryption.
  - Examples: AES, 3DES, ChaCha20
  - Speed: **Fast** (suitable for bulk data)
  - Key distribution problem: Both parties need the same key
  
- **Asymmetric Encryption** uses a **key pair** (public + private).
  - Examples: RSA, ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography)
  - Speed: **Slow** (≈ 1000x slower than symmetric)
  - Used for: Key exchange, digital signatures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid Encryption (used in HTTPS/TLS):** A symmetric session key is exchanged via asymmetric crypto (e.g., RSA or ECDHE), then used for bulk data encryption with AES. This combines the best of both worlds.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $E_K(P)$ | Encryption of plaintext $P$ with key $K$ | Produces ciphertext $C$ |
| $D_K(C)$ | Decryption of ciphertext $C$ with key $K$ | Recovers plaintext $P$ |
| $H(M)$ | Cryptographic hash of message $M$ | Fixed-length output |
| $\text{SHA-256}(M)$ | 256-bit hash output | Output: 64 hex chars |
| $K_{\text{public}}, K_{\text{private}}$ | RSA key pair | $K_{\text{public}} \cdot K_{\text{private}} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ |
| $\text{BCrypt}(P, \text{salt}, c)$ | Password hashing with cost $c$ | Cost $c = 12$ recommended |
| $\text{AES-256}$ block | 16 bytes plaintext | 10/12/14 rounds for 128/192/256-bit keys |
| $\text{entropy}(P)$ | $H = \log_2(N^L)$ | $N$ = charset, $L$ = length |
| $\text{key length} \geq 128$ bits | Minimum for symmetric | NIST SP 800-131A |
| $\text{RSA} \geq 2048$ bits | Minimum for asymmetric | NIST SP 800-57 |
| $\text{PBKDF2 iterations} \geq 10000$ | OWASP recommendation | For SHA-256 |
| $\text{TLS 1.3}$ | Mandatory cipher: **AES-256-GCM** or **ChaCha20-Poly1305** | No CBC mode allowed |

> [!CAUTION]
> When writing KTU exam answers, **never** use the symbols `|` or absolute value bars in tables — always use `\vert` or `\mid`. For example, write $\vert K \vert$ NOT $|K|$.

## 2.3 The Android Keystore System — Why It Matters

The Android Keystore is a **hardware-isolated, system-level service** that:
1. Generates and stores cryptographic keys in a **secure container**
2. Prevents raw key material from being exposed to the application process
3. Supports **StrongBox** (dedicated security chip on supported devices, e.g., Pixel 3+)
4. Enforces **user authentication-bound keys** — keys become unusable until the user authenticates via biometric/PIN
5. Resists extraction even on rooted devices (hardware-backed)

> [!NOTE]
> **StrongBox Keymaster** module is the gold standard — keys are generated and used inside a physically separate chip. KTU often asks: *"Why is hardware-backed key storage preferred over software-only?"*

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Banking Apps (GPay, PhonePe)** | Token-based auth + EncryptedSharedPreferences for cached user data |
| **Healthcare (mHealth)** | HIPAA-compliant encrypted local storage of patient records |
| **Enterprise MDM** | Certificate-based device authentication + app-level encryption |
| **E-commerce** | OAuth 2.0 for "Login with Google", JWT for session management |
| **IoT Mobile Companions** | mTLS for device-to-cloud, biometric for local control |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Password Storage with Salted Hashing (BCrypt)

> **Problem Context:** A user's password `P = "MyP@ssw0rd123"` must be stored such that even if the database is compromised, the original password cannot be recovered, yet the user can be authenticated on subsequent logins.

### Step 1: Generate a Cryptographic Salt
A salt is a **random, unique value** appended to the password before hashing to defeat rainbow-table attacks.

$$S = \text{SecureRandom}(16 \text{ bytes})$$

- Length: **16 bytes = 128 bits** (OWASP recommendation)
- Source: `java.security.SecureRandom` (NOT `java.util.Random` — that's predictable)

### Step 2: Concatenate Salt and Password
$$\text{Input} = P \parallel S = \text{"MyP@ssw0rd123"} \parallel S$$

where $\parallel$ denotes byte-string concatenation.

### Step 3: Apply BCrypt with Cost Factor
The BCrypt algorithm uses the **Blowfish cipher** in a modified form called **EksBlowfish**. The cost factor $c$ controls the iteration count as $2^c$ rounds.

$$H = \text{EksBlowfish}(\text{Input}, c=12)$$

The number of internal iterations:

$$I = 2^{c} = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ iterations}$$

### Step 4: Produce the Final Hash String
BCrypt encodes the algorithm version, cost, salt, and hash into a single string:

$$H_{\text{final}} = \$2a\$12\$<22 \text{ char salt}><31 \text{ char hash}$$

**Total length: 60 characters**

### Step 5: Verification on Login
Given a login attempt $P'$:

1. Extract salt $S$ and cost $c$ from the stored hash
2. Compute $H' = \text{EksBlowfish}(P' \parallel S, c)$
3. Compare $H' \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{final}}$ using **constant-time comparison** (to prevent timing attacks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant-time comparison** ensures the comparison takes the same time regardless of which byte mismatches first, defeating side-channel timing attacks.

### Worked Numerical Example (PBKDF2)

For PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 with the following parameters:
- Password: $P$ (length $L_P$ bytes)
- Salt: $S$ (length 16 bytes)
- Iterations: $I = 10000$
- Derived key length: $dkLen = 32$ bytes (256 bits)

The number of blocks needed:

$$n = \lceil \frac{dkLen}{\text{HMAC output}} \rceil = \lceil \frac{32}{32} \rceil = 1 \text{ block}$$

For each block $i$ (1-indexed):

$$T_i = U_1 \oplus U_2 \oplus \ldots \oplus U_I$$

where:

$$U_1 = \text{HMAC-SHA256}(P, S \parallel \text{INT}(i))$$

$$U_j = \text{HMAC-SHA256}(P, U_{j-1}) \text{ for } j = 2, 3, \ldots, I$$

The final derived key:

$$DK = T_1 \parallel T_2 \parallel \ldots \parallel T_n$$

For our case, $n = 1$:

$$DK = T_1 = \bigoplus_{j=1}^{10000} U_j$$

> [!NOTE]
> Each $U_j$ requires one HMAC-SHA256 computation. Total: **10,000 HMAC calls** to derive a single 256-bit key. This is why PBKDF2 slows down brute-force attacks.

## 3.2 Derivation: AES-256 Encryption Round Structure

The AES-256 cipher processes data in **128-bit (16-byte) blocks** and performs **14 rounds** of transformation for 256-bit keys.

For a single round $r$, the state matrix undergoes four operations:

1. **SubBytes** — Non-linear byte substitution using S-Box:
$$s'_{i,j} = S\text{-Box}(s_{i,j})$$

2. **ShiftRows** — Cyclic left shift of row $i$ by $i$ bytes:
$$s'_{i,j} = s_{i,(j+i) \bmod 4}$$

3. **MixColumns** — Matrix multiplication in $\text{GF}(2^8)$:
$$s'(x) = a(x) \cdot s(x) \bmod p(x)$$

where $p(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$ (AES irreducible polynomial).

4. **AddRoundKey** — XOR with round key $K_r$:
$$s'_{i,j} = s_{i,j} \oplus K_{r}[i,j]$$

The total round structure for AES-256:

$$N_r = \max(N_k, N_b) + 6 = \max(8, 4) + 6 = 14 \text{ rounds}$$

where $N_k = 8$ (key words) and $N_b = 4$ (block words).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The final round (round 14) **omits** the MixColumns step. This is a frequent KTU exam question — *"How many rounds does AES-256 use and which step is missing in the final round?"*

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Authentication Method Comparison

| Method | Security Level | UX Convenience | Offline Support | Hardware-Backed | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Plain Password (no hash)** | Very Low | High | Yes | No | Rare (anti-pattern) |
| **BCrypt-hashed Password** | High | High | Yes | No | High |
| **PBKDF2 + Salt** | High | High | Yes | No | High |
| **BiometricPrompt (Class 3)** | Very High | Very High | Yes (cached) | Yes (TEE) | Very High |
| **OAuth 2.0 + JWT** | High | Very High | No (needs refresh) | N/A | Very High |
| **mTLS with X.509** | Very High | Low | No | Yes (if device cert) | Medium |
| **FIDO2 / WebAuthn** | Highest | High | Yes | Yes | Medium |
| **SMS OTP** | Medium | Medium | No | N/A | Common (legacy) |

## 3.4 JWT Structure — Step-by-Step Decoding

A JWT has three Base64URL-encoded parts separated by dots:

$$\text{JWT} = \underbrace{H}_{\text{Header}} . \underbrace{P}_{\text{Payload}} . \underbrace{S}_{\text{Signature}}$$

**Header (decoded):**
$$\text{Header} = \text{Base64URLDecode}(H) = \{ \text{"alg"}: \text{"HS256"}, \text{"typ"}: \text{"JWT"} \}$$

**Payload (claims):**
$$\text{Payload} = \text{Base64URLDecode}(P) = \{ \text{"sub"}: \text{"user123"}, \text{"iat"}: 1700000000, \text{"exp"}: 1700000900 \}$$

**Signature (HMAC-SHA256):**
$$S = \text{HMAC-SHA256}(\text{secret}, H \parallel \text{"."} \parallel P)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse JWT encoding with encryption. JWT is **encoded (Base64URL), not encrypted**. Anyone can decode the payload. Sensitive data must never be stored in JWT claims without additional encryption (use JWE for that).

## 3.5 Android Keystore Key Generation — Analytical Walkthrough

When `KeyGenParameterSpec` is used to generate an AES-256 key:

$$\text{Key} = \text{Keystore.generateKey}(\text{spec})$$

The key material is stored such that:

$$K_{\text{raw}} \not\in \text{Application Memory}$$

The application receives a **handle (alias)** `K_{\text{alias}} = "my_secret_key"` and uses it via:

$$C = E_{K_{\text{alias}}}(P) = \text{Cipher.getInstance("AES/GCM/NoPadding")}.doFinal(P)$$

Authentication-bound keys require user verification:

$$K_{\text{usable}} \iff \text{UserAuthenticated}(\text{Biometric} \lor \text{PIN})$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Secure Authentication Flow — Login with Biometric + JWT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[App Launch] --> B{User Already Logged In}
    B -->|No| C[Display Login Screen]
    B -->|Yes| D[Check JWT Expiry]
    D -->|Valid| E[Navigate to Home]
    D -->|Expired| F[Silent Token Refresh]
    F -->|Success| E
    F -->|Failure| C
    C --> G[User Enters Credentials]
    G --> H[POST Request to Auth Server over HTTPS]
    H --> I{Server Validates}
    I -->|Invalid| J[Show Error Message]
    J --> C
    I -->|Valid| K[Server Returns Access Token and Refresh Token]
    K --> L[Store Tokens in EncryptedSharedPreferences]
    L --> M[Display BiometricPrompt for App Lock]
    M --> N{User Biometric Auth}
    N -->|Success| E
    N -->|Failure| O[Retry Biometric up to 3 times]
    O -->|Exceeded| P[Fallback to Device PIN]
    P --> E
```

## 4.2 Secure Data Storage Architecture — Layered Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph appLayer["Application Layer"]
        UI[UI Components] --> VM[ViewModel]
        VM --> REPO[Repository Pattern]
    end
    subgraph logicLayer["Security Logic Layer"]
        REPO --> MGR[CryptoManager]
        MGR --> KEYSTORE[Android Keystore]
        MGR --> TINK[Google Tink Crypto Library]
    end
    subgraph storageLayer["Storage Layer"]
        MGR --> ESP[EncryptedSharedPreferences]
        MGR --> EF[EncryptedFile]
        MGR --> SQLC[SQLCipher Database]
        MGR --> CC[Encrypted Room DB Columns]
    end
    subgraph hwLayer["Hardware Layer"]
        KEYSTORE --> TEEHW[Trusted Execution Environment TEE]
        KEYSTORE --> SBX[StrongBox Security Chip]
    end
```

## 4.3 JWT Generation and Verification Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant AuthServer
    participant ResourceServer
    Client->>AuthServer: POST login with username and password
    AuthServer->>AuthServer: Validate credentials against BCrypt hash
    AuthServer->>AuthServer: Generate JWT with claims sub, iat, exp
    AuthServer->>AuthServer: Sign JWT with HMAC-SHA256 secret
    AuthServer-->>Client: Return access token and refresh token
    Client->>Client: Store tokens in EncryptedSharedPreferences
    Client->>ResourceServer: GET protected resource with Authorization Bearer token
    ResourceServer->>ResourceServer: Verify JWT signature and expiry
    alt Valid Token
        ResourceServer-->>Client: Return protected data
    else Invalid Token
        ResourceServer-->>Client: 401 Unauthorized
        Client->>AuthServer: POST refresh token for new access token
        AuthServer-->>Client: New access token
    end
```

## 4.4 AES-256 Encryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plaintext 128 bits] --> SB[SubBytes S-Box]
    SB --> SR[ShiftRows]
    SR --> MC[MixColumns GF 2 to 8]
    MC --> AK[AddRoundKey XOR with K_r]
    AK --> R{Round Counter}
    R -->|Less than 14| SB
    R -->|Round 14| SB2[SubBytes]
    SB2 --> SR2[ShiftRows]
    SR2 --> AK2[AddRoundKey Final]
    AK2 --> C[Ciphertext 128 bits]
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Storage Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Data Type} -->|Small key value| Q2{Sensitive}
    Q1 -->|Large files| Q3{Needs Query}
    Q1 -->|Structured data| Q4{Relational}
    Q2 -->|Yes| ESP[EncryptedSharedPreferences]
    Q2 -->|No| SP[Regular SharedPreferences]
    Q3 -->|Yes| EF[EncryptedFile]
    Q3 -->|No| FS[Internal Storage]
    Q4 -->|Yes| SQLC[SQLCipher or Encrypted Room]
    Q4 -->|No| RDB[Regular Room DB]
```

## 4.6 OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow with PKCE

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant UserAgent
    participant App
    participant AuthProvider
    participant TokenEndpoint
    UserAgent->>App: Initiate Login with Google
    App->>App: Generate code verifier random string
    App->>App: Compute code challenge SHA256 of verifier
    App->>AuthProvider: Authorization request with code challenge
    AuthProvider->>UserAgent: Display login and consent screen
    UserAgent->>AuthProvider: User grants permission
    AuthProvider-->>App: Authorization code
    App->>TokenEndpoint: Exchange code plus code verifier
    TokenEndpoint->>TokenEndpoint: Verify code challenge matches verifier
    TokenEndpoint-->>App: Access token and ID token
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric encryption. State one example algorithm for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Symmetric Encryption | Asymmetric Encryption |
|---|---|---|
| Keys used | Single shared secret key | Key pair: public + private |
| Speed | Fast (bulk data) | Slow (≈ 1000x slower) |
| Key distribution | Challenging (must share securely) | Easy (public key is public) |
| Example | **AES-256** (block cipher) | **RSA-2048** or **ECC** |
| Use case | File encryption, disk encryption | Digital signatures, key exchange |

*Key Points for Full Marks:*
- (1 mark) Correct definition of each
- (1 mark) Valid example algorithm
- (1 mark) Any one distinguishing property (e.g., key count or speed)

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any THREE Android security best practices for storing sensitive data locally.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Use EncryptedSharedPreferences** (from `androidx.security:security-crypto`) for small key-value data — it wraps AES-256-GCM with hardware-backed keys from the Android Keystore.
2. **Never store passwords in plain text** — always use a salted, adaptive hashing algorithm such as **BCrypt (cost ≥ 12)** or **Argon2id**.
3. **Use the Android Keystore System** to generate and store cryptographic keys, preventing key material from being extracted from the device memory.

*Acceptable alternative answers:*
- Disable `android:allowBackup="false"` in the manifest to prevent ADB-based data extraction
- Use Certificate Pinning for network traffic
- Apply ProGuard/R8 obfuscation to prevent reverse engineering
- Use `FLAG_SECURE` to prevent screenshots of sensitive screens
